title: Banks quick to act on negative gearing
full_text:
BUDGET 2026 Australia’s banking sector has started tightening its policies around negative gearing following the federal government’s move to limit it to new investment properties only from mid-2027.
The nation’s fifth-largest lender, Macquarie Bank, told mortgage brokers on Monday that negative gearing would no longer be factored into its serviceability calculations for established property purchases made after last week’s budget, while Westpac has warned its lenders that policy changes may be looming.
Mortgage brokers expect other lenders to follow and forecast a big reduction in investor home loans. They believe future first-home buyers will struggle to compete for new builds with investors, who are still allowed to negatively gear their existing properties.
Negative gearing allows investors to claim tax losses when their costs – such as interest, insurance and management fees – exceed rental income, delivering them a bigger tax refund. For some it is the only affordable way to get into the housing market.
Macquarie told mortgage brokers that for contracts executed after May 12, budget night, negative gearing would “only be factored into serviceability calculations if the property meets the definition of new build and generally adds to housing supply”.
Westpac was reported to have also informed mortgage brokers that it was changing lending policies around negative gearing, but a spokesman said it had not changed anything “at this stage” and had not communicated any changes to mortgage brokers.
“However, we have advised our home lenders there may be changes in the future,” a Westpac spokesman said.
Oracle Lending Solutions managing director Angelo Benedetti said “there’s rumours that other banks will follow shortly”.
“Macquarie has the biggest growth in home loan lending and everybody watches Macquarie,” he said. “Every lender and bank is trying to copy what Macquarie do so I think all the lenders will be going down the path – once one starts it’s the domino effect.
“Nobody wants to be the first cab off the rank, but Macquarie can afford to do that because they have so much business coming through. Once it’s full-steam ahead and banks start imposing new restrictions, it’s going to have a massive impact going forward on people’s borrowing capacity and affordability.” Mr Benedetti said the budget’s negative gearing changes would push up new building costs and hurt first-home buyers’. “The young kid trying to buy a new home is competing with someone who has got the extra tax incentives and more money and maybe a couple more properties,” he said.
summary: Australia's banks are tightening rules on negative gearing after the government announced it will only apply to new investment properties from mid-2027. Macquarie Bank has already stopped considering negative gearing for established properties, leading to expectations of reduced investor loans and challenges for first-home buyers.
author: Anthony Keane
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Negative gearing changes announced in the 2026 budget.
Macquarie Bank will not factor negative gearing for established properties after May 12, 2026.
Westpac hints at future policy changes but has not made any yet.
Other banks are expected to follow Macquarie's lead.
Changes may increase costs for new builds and affect first-home buyers.
ticker_line: Banks tighten rules on negative gearing investments
